title: Inside a T. Rex Bone: Blood Vessels That Survived 66 Million Years
summary:
For decades, scientists have searched for dinosaur DNA with no success. But soft tissues—like blood vessels—can survive in fossils, and they reveal far more than DNA ever could. A team led by Jerit Mitchell, a physics PhD candidate at the University of Regina, has identified preserved blood vessels inside a rib of Scotty, the largest T. rex ever discovered. Their findings were published in Scientific Reports.

Scotty, housed at the Royal Saskatchewan Museum in Canada, lived a brutal life around 66 million years ago. Many of its bones show signs of injury, possibly from combat or disease. One rib stands out: a large fracture that had only partially healed. When bones are damaged, the body increases blood vessel activity to support healing. The structures Mitchell's team observed appear to be part of that process—a dense network of mineralized vessels.

Studying the inside of fossil bones is challenging. Standard CT scanners can't penetrate the dense, mineralized structure. So the team turned to synchrotron light, a powerful form of high-intensity X-rays produced at particle accelerator facilities. This technique allowed them to visualize tiny internal features with remarkable clarity and analyze the chemical composition. The vessels had been preserved as iron-rich mineralized casts, appearing in two distinct layers that reflect a complex environmental history.

"The partially healed fracture offers a rare opportunity to study how a T. rex recovered from injury," Mitchell said. By examining these blood vessels, researchers can gain insight into healing processes and survival strategies in large predatory dinosaurs. This work may also provide a basis for comparison with modern animals like birds, which are closely related to dinosaurs.

The findings could guide future fossil discoveries: bones that show signs of injury or disease may be more likely to preserve soft tissues. With the combination of physics, paleontology, and advanced imaging, scientists are uncovering details about dinosaur biology once thought impossible to study.
why_it_matters: Understanding how dinosaurs healed from injuries and preserved soft tissues helps scientists reconstruct their biology and behavior, and provides a basis for comparing ancient and modern animal physiology — insights that could also inform medical research on bone healing.

keywords.explanation: Parts of an organism's body that are not bone, such as blood vessels, skin, or muscles.
The genetic material of dinosaurs, which scientists have searched for but never found intact.
A break or crack in a bone, often caused by injury.
Blood vessels that have been replaced by minerals over time, preserving their shape as hard casts.
A very powerful type of X-ray produced by particle accelerators, used to see tiny details inside objects.
A large machine that speeds up tiny particles to create high-energy beams, used for research and imaging.

questions.question: What is the name of the largest T. rex ever discovered?
What technique did the researchers use to study the inside of the fossil bone?
What did the preserved blood vessels appear as?
Why did the rib bone have increased blood vessel activity?
According to Jerit Mitchell, what does the fracture offer?
options: A rare opportunity to study how a T. rex recovered from injury
A chance to extract dinosaur DNA
Clues to the extinction of dinosaurs
questions.correct_answer: A rare opportunity to study how a T. rex recovered from injury

background_read: For decades, scientists have attempted to extract dinosaur DNA from fossils but have never succeeded because DNA breaks down over millions of years. However, soft tissues like blood vessels can sometimes be preserved as mineralized casts.

The specimen studied is Scotty (a Tyrannosaurus rex discovered in Saskatchewan, Canada in 1991, housed at the Royal Saskatchewan Museum). Scotty is the largest T. rex ever found, with a skeleton over 66 million years old.

The research team used synchrotron light (a high-intensity X-ray produced at particle accelerator facilities) at a Canadian light source facility. This technique can penetrate dense fossil bone and reveal microstructures invisible to standard CT scanners.

This work builds on earlier discoveries by paleontologist Mary Schweitzer, who detected flexible blood vessels in a T. rex fossil in 2005, sparking debate about soft tissue preservation over deep time.
Article_Structure: LEAD: No dinosaur DNA found, but blood vessels survive and reveal more than DNA could
└─ KEY SPECIMEN: Scotty, the largest T. rex, housed at Royal Saskatchewan Museum
├─ WHO: Team led by Jerit Mitchell (physics PhD candidate, University of Regina)
├─ WHEN: Lived about 66 million years ago; findings published in Scientific Reports
└─ DETAIL: One rib shows a large partially healed fracture
METHOD: Used synchrotron light (intense X-rays from particle accelerators) instead of standard CT scanners
└─ FINDING: Dense network of mineralized blood vessels preserved as iron-rich casts, in two distinct layers
IMPLICATIONS: Provides insight into how T. rex recovered from injury; guides future fossil discovery
└─ QUOTE: 'The partially healed fracture offers a rare opportunity to study how a T. rex recovered from injury' — Mitchell
FUTURE: Comparison with modern birds (dinosaur relatives); combination of physics, paleontology, and advanced imaging

perspectives.description: The use of synchrotron light opens up a new way to study fossils, revealing structures that were previously invisible and could lead to many more discoveries about dinosaur biology.
The preserved structures are mineralized casts, not original organic tissue, so they cannot provide DNA or detailed cellular information. The study is limited to bones with signs of injury.
This research demonstrates the power of interdisciplinary approaches (physics + paleontology) but relies on expensive and rare particle accelerator facilities, limiting widespread application.

Future work may identify other fossils with soft tissue preservation, enabling more detailed studies of dinosaur physiology and even comparisons with modern birds, potentially revealing evolutionary patterns.
